narrativeContexts.narrative: "I don't believe in altruism." Feyd handed over the payment anyway. A household servant accepted it without commenting on the contradiction.
The gift was wrapped in ceremony. Jessica presented it. A household servant received it. Neither called it what it was.

The balance sheet did not care about sentiment. Gaius studied it anyway, looking for the gap between what was recorded and what was true. Gaius performed the motions. The feeling behind them was somewhere else.
The expenditure was approved through proper channels. The channels existed to protect Rabban, not to slow him down.
The expenditure was approved through proper channels. The channels existed to protect Tyros, not to slow her down.
Armand explained the scheme over wine. The wine was better than the ethics. He drank both.
The expenditure was approved through proper channels. The channels existed to protect Casy, not to slow her down.
Vladimir delivered the command in person. No written record. No intermediaries. If this went wrong, only two people would know exactly how.
Shaddam gave generously and counted the cost. Every gift created an obligation. He would pay — not today, but the ledger was updated.
Tyros presented the facts in order. The leadership received them in order. The emotional content of the report was a separate matter — handled privately by both parties.
"Nothing good to report." Armand said it cheerfully, which made it worse.
Leto spread the expenditure across three budgets. Not hidden — organized. The distinction mattered to the auditor. It did not matter to the outcome.
The money moved. The information followed. Paul and A household servant were past pretending this was anything other than what it was.
Protocol demanded certain forms. Gurney and Jessica observed all of them while ignoring the spirit entirely.
Feyd chose the truth that would damage most. Not a lie — lies can be denied. Rabban had no defense against accuracy.
Irulan observed Ahsir's reaction with clinical interest. The provocation was data. Ahsir's response was the experiment.
Hasimir said nothing offensive. The offense was in what was left unsaid — the space between the words where Tyros filled in the insult.
The expenditure was approved through proper channels. The channels existed to protect Jessica, not to slow her down.
Vladimir sketched the plan without naming names. Names could be traced. The architecture of the thing was enough — he could fill in the blanks, and if anyone asked, he had said nothing specific.
Shaddam praised Ahsir the way one might praise a well-made tool. Accurate. Appreciative. Utterly devoid of warmth.
Edric sketched the plan without naming names. Names could be traced. The architecture of the thing was enough — he could fill in the blanks, and if anyone asked, he had said nothing specific.
Gaius shared the information precisely. Not gossip — intelligence. The distinction was that he intended to benefit from sharing it. Gaius checked the door. Twice.
Rabban shared the information like a transaction. The vulnerability was tactical. Vladimir recognized this and valued it anyway.
The transaction was conducted through intermediaries and euphemisms. Tyros paid. A household servant collected. Everyone maintained deniability.
The money moved. The information followed. Thufir and Duncan were past pretending this was anything other than what it was.
Armand set the message down. He did not read it again. Some news only needs to land once.
Casy reviewed the day's correspondence. Nothing required a response. The silence from outside was louder than any message.
A gift. A gesture. A mutual understanding dressed in the language of generosity. Piter and Vladimir both read between the lines.
The investment was calculated. Leto had run the projections three times — not from doubt, but from the professional paranoia of anyone who understood markets.
The price of doing business in Harkonnen. Feyd paid it, and resented it, and would pay it again tomorrow.
"The beauty of this plan," Hasimir said, "is that no one will believe we planned it." he almost smiled. Almost.

The transaction was conducted through intermediaries and euphemisms. Iakin paid. A household servant collected. Everyone maintained deniability.
Casy signed the authorization without reading it twice. Fenring did not deal in hesitation.
Gurney delivered the report without embellishment. The leadership listened with the particular attention of someone who understood that what was left unsaid mattered more.
The gift was presented with ceremony. Shaddam gave. He received. The hierarchy was reinforced by the direction of generosity.
"Consider it a gesture of goodwill." Hasimir's smile suggested the goodwill had an expiration date.
Edric signed without pleasure. Wealth was a tool, not a reward. The tool was being applied correctly.
Paul named the price. A household servant did not negotiate. This was an acquisition, and they both knew which side was being acquired.

Shaddam paid in small amounts through multiple channels. No single transaction would draw attention. The pattern would, but by then it wouldn't matter. The action was correct. That was all that could be said for it.
Three factions. One misunderstanding. Edric would supply the misunderstanding. The factions would supply their own ruin.
Rabban reviewed the day's correspondence. Nothing required a response. The silence from outside was louder than any message.
Thufir waited. Not patiently — he had simply run out of things to control. The stillness was strategic.

"I don't believe in altruism." Feyd handed over the payment anyway. Vladimir accepted it without commenting on the contradiction.
The investment was calculated. Irulan had run the projections three times — not from doubt, but from the professional paranoia of anyone who understood markets.
"An investment in the future." Hasimir said it the way people say things they don't quite believe. The money moved regardless.
The balance sheet did not care about sentiment. Vladimir studied it anyway, looking for the gap between what was recorded and what was true.
Thufir reviewed the figures alone. The numbers told a story the ledgers wouldn't repeat outside this room. Atreides's position was shifting.
Armand counted the cost and decided it was acceptable. The cost wasn't just financial. He knew that too.

The command was a single sentence. Leto did not elaborate. Elaboration implied the order was open for discussion.
"My advice is worth exactly what you paid for it." Feyd gave it anyway. The accuracy stung.
Vladimir gave the order twice, rephrasing the second time to ensure the wording couldn't be exploited.
Shaddam delivered the command in person. No written record. No intermediaries. If this went wrong, only two people would know exactly how.
Tyros spoke carefully, placing each word like a stone in a wall. A superior could accept the wall or watch it fall.
Ahsir presented the advice as analysis. The recommendation buried in the last paragraph was the point. A superior understood this.
Irulan shared what she knew. Not everything — enough to be useful, not enough to be dangerous. She recognized the calibration.

The words were chosen the way a surgeon chooses an incision — precise, necessary, and deeply felt by the patient.
Leto named the price. Jessica did not negotiate. This was an acquisition, and they both knew which side was being acquired.
Irulan received the news without expression. The reaction would come later, privately, in decisions that no one would connect to this moment.
"Consider it a gesture of goodwill." Hasimir's smile suggested the goodwill had an expiration date. Again. Hasimir approached it with the same careful attention. The familiarity didn't dull it.
Acknowledgment, not affection. Vladimir gave Kasha credit for competence. The bar was set at 'functional' and Kasha had cleared it.

Edric stood at the window. Nothing required his attention. The inattention itself was a kind of attention — he was watching the gaps between events.
The proposal was delivered in writing. Rabban believed in documentation. He believed in plausible deniability. Both were served.

Kasha listed the consequences the way a clerk lists inventory. Comprehensive. Emotionless. Devastating.
The order was given in Shaddam's voice but carried out by other hands. This was the architecture of power — distance between decision and consequence. Shaddam hadn't smiled in days.
"Consider it a gesture of goodwill." Hasimir's smile suggested the goodwill had an expiration date. Again. Hasimir approached it with the same careful attention. The familiarity didn't dull it.
